readings.source: CME Urea Granular FOB Egypt Jul-26 contract settled ~$387.50/t on 10 Jul (down ~18% from the $470–480 range of 7–8 Jul), extending the slide from the post-May spike. Egypt switched on 7 Jul from a fixed $90/t export duty to a 10% ad valorem charge (BC Insight, 7 Jul 2026), reducing the levy burden at lower price points and adding supply-side pressure. Level for 14–15 Jul not independently confirmed — subscription-grade assessments (Argus/ICIS) required for confirmed physical level.

July Yara–Mosaic Tampa CFR contract confirmed at ~$800/t — up $25/t from June's $775/t — per QCIntel, consistent with the North African supply-tightening signal in the prior week's note. Monthly benchmark — paywalled for full confirmed assessment.

Holding prior CME DAP FOB NOLA indication (~$757.50/st, 7–8 Jul); no clear fresh public citation for the week of 14–15 Jul. Aug $740, Sep $737.50 on the curve. Indicative — not a confirmed physical assessment.
No fresh public citations as of 15 Jul — holding prior indications (~$405–415/t CFR). Not exchange-traded; subscription-only for confirmed level.

readings.wow: rising sharply — 3-month high on Hormuz re-escalation
readings.source: Front-month ~€52.90/MWh on 15 Jul, hitting a 3-month high as President Trump ordered resumption of port blockades on Iran and imposed a 20% security fee on Strait of Hormuz cargo, reigniting LNG supply risk. Prices rallied >5% on 14 Jul (~€51.4/MWh) and extended gains on 15 Jul; EU storage refill is under pressure from heat-wave demand and remains below-average (~51% vs ~60% a year ago). Up from ~€48/MWh on 10 Jul and above last week's ~€50 spike high.

editorialReading: Gas is now the dominant story — and the numbers have moved sharply. TTF front-month surged to a 3-month high near €52.90/MWh on 15 July after President Trump ordered resumed port blockades on Iran and imposed a 20% security fee on Strait of Hormuz cargo, firing a direct LNG supply-risk signal for Europe. With EU storage still running well below year-ago levels (~51% vs ~60%), the timing is acutely uncomfortable. The July Tampa ammonia contract has been confirmed at ~$800/t (up $25/t on June's $775/t), consistent with last week's North African supply-tightening signal. Urea, by contrast, has retreated: Egypt FOB granular fell from the $470–480/t rebound back to around $385–400/t by 10 July, after Egypt switched on 7 July from a fixed $90/t export duty to a 10% ad valorem charge — easing the burden on exporters at lower price points and releasing more supply. DAP holds near $755–760/st and potash is stable at $405–415/t. Above €50/MWh, BASF, Yara and OCI face renewed pressure to idle EU ammonia plants and import finished product — the chronic TTF-driven cost squeeze is re-accelerating at the exact moment the acute war premium on urea is softening. These two forces are working in opposite directions, and gas is now winning.
watchNext: Whether TTF sustains above €50/MWh into the autumn 2026 application window — if so, EU plant-idling pressure returns and import dependence deepens. Whether August Tampa ammonia confirms above July's ~$800/t on continued North African tightening. Whether urea finds a floor at ~$385–400/t or slides further as Egypt's new ad valorem duty adds more supply. India / China urea tender activity and any export curbs remain the physical swing factor. Sourcing note: real-time Argus / ICIS / Green Markets assessments are subscription-only and the free World Bank Pink Sheet lags ~a month — these are publicly-cited indications.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
